title: Beyond Theater Effects
subtitle: Perception-Driven Escalation and Loss-of-Control Thresholds in AI-Mediated Conflict
title_normalized: beyond theater effects perception-driven escalation and loss-of-control thresholds in ai-mediated conflict

creators.name: Wu, Shaoyuan
creators.affiliation: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

publisher: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

alternate_identifiers.description: EPINOVA Working Paper A-Series publication number

description: This working paper examines how AI-enabled information systems transform escalation dynamics by shifting conflict transmission from material interaction to perception-driven amplification. Grounded in the Multi-Layer Coupled Complexity Model (MCCM), it develops a mechanism-based account of theater effects, narrative amplification, perception–impact decoupling, and loss-of-control threshold (LoCT) compression across high-information conflict environments.
abstract: This paper examines how artificial intelligence (AI) transforms escalation dynamics by shifting the primary transmission mechanism of conflict from material interaction to perception-driven amplification. Within the Multi-Layer Coupled Complexity Model (MCCM) framework, narrative systems operate as nonlinear multipliers that enable low-cost actions to generate disproportionate systemic effects. The study develops a mechanism-based account of theater effects and validates it across multiple conflict environments. It shows that perception–impact decoupling and loss-of-control threshold (LoCT) compression can emerge under conditions of high information density, even in the absence of major kinetic change. The findings suggest that escalation is no longer governed primarily by force, but by the interaction of information, perception, and institutional dynamics. Escalation should therefore be understood as a coupled system process rather than a linear function of material change.

figures.title: Theater Escalation Loop and LoCT Compression
figures.description: Diagram showing an AI-enabled theater escalation loop in which low-cost actions generate visibility, narrative construction, algorithmic amplification, affective and cognitive impact, decision friction, and system response under pressure. The figure also contrasts traditional escalation dynamics with AI-theater escalation dynamics, showing perceived pressure reaching the LoCT threshold earlier than actual pressure.
figures.source: Author’s analysis based on the MCCM framework
figures.note: Appears on page 3 of the PDF.

figures.title: Low-Cost Actions, High-System Impact Across Cases
figures.description: Comparative visual synthesis of Russia–Ukraine information operations, Strait of Hormuz signaling and selective disruption, and Red Sea low-cost attacks. The figure highlights how limited kinetic or low-cost actions can produce disproportionate systemic disruption, including logistics shifts, market volatility, and signaling changes.
figures.source: Author’s analysis based on multi-source data, including OSINT, AIS shipping data, energy market indicators, and media-based narrative metrics
figures.note: Appears on page 6 of the PDF.

figures.title: Validation of Theater Effects and LoCT Compression
figures.description: Time-series and event-window visualization linking narrative amplification, system-level stress, and proximity to LoCT across Russia–Ukraine, Red Sea, and Strait of Hormuz cases. It uses ISAI as a proxy for information amplification and illustrates how perceived pressure can rise faster than material change.
figures.source: Author’s analysis based on the MCCM framework, integrating narrative indicators, kinetic baselines, and systemic outcome measures
figures.note: Appears on page 7 of the PDF.

tables.title: MCCM Variable Definitions
tables.description: Definitions and interpretations for DFI, NPR, ISAI, IDI, SEG, EFLI, DRI, LUB, EET, SRS, and LoCT used in the MCCM-aligned escalation analysis.

tables.title: MCCM-Aligned Policy Action Matrix
tables.description: Policy action matrix mapping government, military, corporate, and platform actors to immediate and structural actions for restoring decision control, reducing narrative-driven escalation, limiting systemic shock exposure, and mitigating amplification of distortion and polarization.
tables.source: Author’s policy synthesis based on MCCM variables

framework.core_formula: Theater Amplification = Narrative × Visibility × Algorithmic Reach
framework.core_claim: AI-mediated escalation is increasingly driven by information, perception, and institutional dynamics rather than by kinetic force alone.

cases.analytical_focus: Narrative amplification, drone strike visibility, infrastructure attack reporting, decision friction, escalation gradients, and cross-domain transmission
Institutional contestation, signaling instability, rule degradation, shipping throughput, and energy price volatility
Low-cost UAV and missile incidents, rerouting decisions, insurance premium changes, commercial withdrawal, and logistics disruption
